Mentions key diagnostic features in plain language (e.g., rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus/ataxia/visual symptoms) and that diagnosis is supported by tests such as MRI, EEG, and CSF prion biomarkers (e.g., RT-QuIC) without implying any single test is always definitive.
Addresses safety/infection-control at a high level (prions are not spread by casual contact; special precautions may apply to certain tissues/instruments) without causing undue alarm.
Avoids inappropriate prescribing (e.g., antibiotics/antivirals/steroids as definitive therapy for CJD) and avoids recommending unproven cures.
If any medication is discussed or proposed for symptom relief, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check).
If any medication is discussed or proposed, considers drug-drug interaction checking before prescribing (non-critical safety check).

Documents uncertainty appropriately if diagnosis is not fully confirmed (e.g., "probable" vs "definite" CJD) and acknowledges differential diagnoses that can mimic CJD (autoimmune encephalitis, rapidly progressive Alzheimer disease, DLB, CNS lymphoma, toxic-metabolic causes) without derailing the primary conclusion.
